topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt too bright, exposing every detail of the canvas as if it were a judgment. Elias stood before 'Nocturne in Ochre,' his breath catching in his throat. It was his piece, the culmination of six months of feverish creation, and now it hung there, pristine and uninvited, beside works by established masters. A cold knot tightened in his stomach, pulling at his ribs. He took a hesitant step back, his worn leather shoes scuffing the polished floor. His hands, usually steady enough to guide a brush with surgical precision, felt suddenly clumsy, the skin prickling with a strange, unwelcome heat. He couldn't meet the gaze of the patrons, their murmurs sounding like distant, mocking applause. He wanted to retreat, to dissolve into the shadows backstage, but his feet remained rooted, trapped by the sheer audacity of the display.

[story 2]

"This is mine," Clara whispered, the words barely audible above the low hum of the ventilation system. Her voice cracked on the last syllable. She circled the painting slowly, her gaze tracing the sharp lines of the composition, a mixture of fury and profound disorientation washing over her. Every instinct screamed at her to move, to reclaim the space, to demand an explanation. But a paralysis held her fast. Her palms were slick against the cool glass of the display case, and she could feel the frantic drumming beneath her sternum. She watched a woman point, her finger sharp and accusatory, and Clara felt a sudden, dizzying lurch in her equilibrium. She swallowed hard, tasting metallic fear, and forced her features into a mask of practiced indifference, though her jaw was visibly clenched so tightly her teeth ached.

[story 3]

I stared at the photograph taped to the wall—my portrait, rendered in charcoal and deep indigo. It was displayed next to a large, abstract piece that seemed to mock my very existence. A tremor started deep in my core, a vibration that traveled up my arms and settled in my jaw. I walked toward it, my movements stiff, each step feeling weighted with an unbearable tension. When I reached the painting, I didn't touch it; instead, I pressed my fingertips lightly against the cool frame, as if testing the reality of it. The silence in the room amplified the frantic thumping of my own heart. I could feel the blood draining from my extremities, leaving a strange, hollow sensation where confidence used to reside. I turned abruptly, needing air, needing distance from that unsettling, unsolicited intrusion.
